Volume 92 · 2026 · 104759

Bitcoin Student Research Prize (BSRP) 2026  ·  Original Research Article

Who Considers, Who Owns? Multi-Study Evidence on the Behavioral Process of Cryptocurrency Adoption in the United States

Erin B. Fitz  ·  Kyle L. Saunders
Department of Political Science, Colorado State University, 1782 Campus Delivery, Fort Collins, CO, 80523, USA
Correspondence: erin.fitz@colostate.edu (E.B. Fitz)  ·  kyle.saunders@colostate.edu (K.L. Saunders)
Received: 25 September 2025 Revised: 25 January 2026 Accepted: 1 February 2026 Available online: 28 February 2026

Keywords

  • Bitcoin
  • Ethereum
  • Cryptocurrency
  • Theory of Planned Behavior
  • Innovation adoption
  • Operational ideology
  • Need for Chaos
  • Personality
  • Digital assets
  • Risk

Abstract

Cryptocurrency is a rapidly expanding landscape of digital assets spanning sociopolitical, psychological, technological, and financial domains. Although prior research identifies factors associated with owning cryptocurrency, most work overlooks the prerequisite considering stage. We draw on the Theory of Planned Behavior to examine whether individuals who would consider owning cryptocurrency differ from owners and those who reject the possibility of owning outright. Using three independent, nationally representative samples of US adults (n=803; n=1908; n=2086) and a modeling approach that allows us to compare the three groups, we find that roughly one in five respondents do not own, but would consider owning, cryptocurrency. While both considerers and owners share similar demographics and personality traits, considerers are not like owners in all ways or to the same degree: Conservative political ideology and technological optimism relate more strongly to considering than owning, whereas stock ownership and disruptive orientations (the "Need for Chaos") relate more strongly to owning than considering. Our data also mirror aggregate market patterns, with most respondents considering and owning Bitcoin (followed by Ethereum). Accounting for these differences is essential for understanding what cryptocurrency represents to various individuals, as well as effective communication, segmentation, and policy design.

~1 in 5
US adults would consider owning crypto (but don't yet)
3
independent nationally representative samples
4,797
total analytic respondents across all studies
$2.5T
crypto market cap at time of writing (Feb 2026)

1. Introduction

Since the emergence of Bitcoin in 2008, the cryptocurrency landscape has rapidly evolved amid a torrent of digital assets—more than 31 million coins and tokens collectively valued at nearly 2.5 trillion US dollars as of February 2026. While Bitcoin and Ethereum consistently account for nearly three-quarters of the total market capitalization, a growing set of alternative cryptocurrencies make up the rest of the sector, varying widely in purpose, viability, and price.

Previous research suggests that between 14% and 30% of US adults own or have owned cryptocurrency at some point, and that owners differ from non-owners across sociopolitical, psychological, technological, and financial domains. Yet most extant literature focuses only on existing cryptocurrency owners—an approach that risks conflating the factors associated with end-stage behavior with those associated with the prerequisite considering stage.

Attitude-behavior theories like the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) posit that considering precedes intention and intention precedes taking action, but neither considering nor intention guarantee behavioral follow-through. We ask: Which factors are associated with considering cryptocurrency adoption, as similar to or distinct from those associated with rejecting and owning?

Core Argument The TPB contends that all owners were once considerers, but not all considerers become owners. By this view, we might expect considerers to share at least some similarities with owners, though not necessarily in all ways or to the same degree. Focusing only on ownership risks providing a fragmented and potentially incomplete picture of who might engage with cryptocurrency.

2. Existing Evidence on Cryptocurrency Adoption

Prior evidence finds that cryptocurrency owners differ from non-owners across multiple domains. Demographically, owners tend to be younger, male, non-white and/or Hispanic, and have higher levels of education, income, and religiosity. With regard to personality, owners are often more open to experience, less conscientious, and more risk-tolerant.

Emerging work also highlights potential differences in personality adaptations. Owners often score lower on authoritarianism and higher in anti-establishment orientations like Need for Chaos—the expressed desire to dismantle existing social and political systems amid periods of real and perceived marginalization. Willingness to take financial risks and other investment behaviors are also associated with ownership.

2.1. Considering Cryptocurrency

Despite these insights, most existing research focuses only on those who own cryptocurrency. According to the TPB, considerations about a behavior precede intentions and intentions precede action, but neither guarantee behavior will occur. Studies concerned primarily with owning cryptocurrency can only provide insights into which factors relate to following through with adoption—not why individuals might consider (or not) adopting in the first place.

2.2. The Present Study

We operationalize cryptocurrency engagement as an ordinal variable and use generalized ordered logit (partial proportional odds) models to examine whether independent variables elucidated by previous ownership research are consistent for both considerers and owners, or vary in strength. We test three alternative hypotheses:

Research Hypotheses
H1
Owners are distinct from all non-owners, including considerers.
H2
Despite both being non-owners, considerers are distinct from rejectors.
H3
To the extent that considerers are distinct from rejectors, considerers are not like owners in all ways or to the same degree.

3. Data and Measures

We collected three original, nationally representative, cross-sectional samples of non-institutionalized US adults (aged 18+). All three studies include the same measures but draw on independent, non-overlapping samples collected from different providers at different points in time—allowing us to assess whether results are consistent across samples by treating each study as an independent test.

Table 1. Summary of Studies
Study Source Protocol Dates Fielded Total n Analytic n
1 Cooperative Election Study Online probability sample matched on voter registration, age, race, gender, education Oct 1 – Dec 10, 2024 831 803
2 Lucid Online non-probability sample matched on age, gender, ethnicity, region Feb 14 – Mar 1, 2025 1,932 1,908
3 Verasight Online multimode sample matched on age, gender, ethnicity, income, education, region, partisanship Jun 10 – Jun 14, 2025 2,128 2,086

Note: Analytic n represents respondents included after accounting for attrition, missing values on key variables, and failed attention checks. Target population: non-institutionalized US adults (aged 18+).

3.2. Dependent Variable

Respondents were asked: "Do you personally, or jointly with a spouse, own any type of cryptocurrency, such as Bitcoin or Ethereum?" Non-owners were then asked whether they would ever consider owning. From these responses, we created a three-point ordinal variable:

Rejectors
46–68%
Does not own and would not consider owning cryptocurrency
Considerers
19–22%
Does not own but would consider owning cryptocurrency
Owners
13–32%
Currently owns cryptocurrency (any type)

3.3. Independent Variables

Drawing on prior literature, we include measures across seven domains:

  • Sociodemographics: Age, Male gender, White race, Hispanic ethnicity, Education, Income, Religiosity
  • Big Five Personality: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism
  • Political Orientation: Operational Ideology (averaged policy preferences across 4 issues)
  • Personality Adaptations: Need for Chaos, Authoritarianism, Need for Cognition
  • Trust: Trust Government, Trust Banks, Trust People
  • Technological Optimism: Supports Developing AI
  • Investments & Risk: Stock Owner, Investing Risk (DOSPERT scale)

4. Results

4.1. Finding 1: Rates of Rejecting, Considering, and Owning

Approximately 13% of Study 1 and 18% of Study 2 report owning cryptocurrency. The proportion of owners in Study 3 is markedly higher (approximately 32%), in line with evidence that roughly a third of Americans have owned or used cryptocurrency at some point. We also find that roughly one-fifth of each sample would consider owning cryptocurrency (19%, 20%, and 22% in Studies 1–3), leaving 46%, 62%, and 68% who neither own nor would consider owning.

4.2. Finding 2: Types of Cryptocurrencies Considered and Owned

Most considerers indicated they would consider Bitcoin (93%, 89%, and 93%), followed by Ethereum (37%, 31%, and 37%). A similar pattern emerges among owners, with most indicating they owned Bitcoin (70%, 76%, and 87%), followed by Ethereum (34%, 33%, and 53%). These self-reported rates mirror aggregate market data, lending external validity to our measures. A sizeable minority of both considerers (24–35%) and owners (19–49%) report interest in or ownership of more than one type of cryptocurrency.

4.3. Finding 3: Owning vs. Considering and Considering vs. Rejecting

Several independent variables align with patterns documented in extant literature: Younger Age, higher Income, greater Trust Government, greater Investing Risk, and greater Need for Chaos are directionally consistent in all samples and significantly associated with ownership in two or more samples.

For the probability of considering versus rejecting, younger Age, greater Openness, lesser Need for Cognition, more conservative Operational Ideology, lesser Need for Chaos, greater Supports Developing AI, and greater Investing Risk distinguish considerers from rejectors in two or more samples. We find strong support for both H1 (owners differ from non-owners) and H2 (considerers differ from rejectors).

4.4. Finding 4: Differences Between Rejectors, Considerers, and Owners (H3)

Brant tests confirm that the proportional odds assumption is violated for key variables—most notably Need for Chaos and Stock Owner (in all three studies), and Operational Ideology and Supports Developing AI (in Studies 2 and 3). This justifies a generalized ordered logit (partial proportional odds) model.

Key Results from Partial Proportional Odds Models More conservative Operational Ideology and greater Supports Developing AI are more strongly related to considering than owning (in 2 of 3 studies). Conversely, identifying as a Stock Owner and greater Need for Chaos are more strongly related to owning than considering (in 2+ studies). Variables including Age, Income, Openness, Conscientiousness, Religiosity, and Investing Risk operate uniformly across both thresholds.

4.5. Average Marginal Effects (Study 3)

Average marginal effects (AMEs) clarify the substantive size of predicted effects when each variable moves from its minimum to maximum values, holding all else constant.

Table 3. Average Marginal Effects (Study 3)
Variable Would Not Consider Would Consider Owns PO Assumption
Age +0.24*** −0.04*** −0.20*** Meets
Male −0.08*** +0.01** +0.07*** Meets
Income −0.11* +0.02* +0.09* Meets
Religiosity −0.17*** +0.03*** +0.14*** Meets
Openness −0.16** +0.03** +0.13** Meets
Conscientiousness +0.17** −0.03** −0.14** Meets
Investing Risk −0.32*** +0.05*** +0.27*** Meets
Operational Ideology −0.15*** +0.10*** +0.05 n.s. Violates
Need for Chaos −0.00 n.s. −0.11*** +0.11* Violates
Supports Developing AI −0.12*** +0.09*** +0.02 n.s. Violates
Stock Owner −0.05 n.s. −0.07*** +0.12*** Violates

Note: Average marginal effects estimated from generalized ordered logit models in Study 3. Bold values highlight where the variable operates differently across thresholds. *p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001.

5. Discussion

Across three identical analyses using independent samples, we find that owners differ from non-owners (H1) and considerers differ from rejectors (H2); however, considerers are not like owners in all ways or to the same degree (H3). While factors like age, gender, and openness to experience relate to both considering and owning, more conservative operational ideology and support for developing AI are more strongly associated with considering than owning. At the same time, stock ownership and Need for Chaos are more strongly associated with owning than considering.

Challenge to Prior Research Variables previously dismissed as inconsequential (e.g., operational ideology) do, in fact, matter—just not necessarily at the ownership stage. Likewise, disruptive orientations (e.g., Need for Chaos) primarily arise at the ownership, but not the considering, stage. Investigating these differences simultaneously in multivariate models is necessary to identify how a certain segment of a population is related to a given adoption stage.

In our data, investing risk consistently yields the largest average marginal effect, reducing rejection and increasing ownership, all else equal. This suggests that the strongest determinant of actually owning cryptocurrency is perceived behavioral control vis-à-vis financial risk—individuals must believe cryptocurrency is both worth considering and realistically within their capabilities to adopt.

5.1. Theoretical Implications

Situating our individual-level data in the diffusion theory model suggests that, despite sample differences in self-reported ownership rates (13%–32%), cryptocurrency adoption in the United States is in or nearing the early majority phase.

5.2. Managerial Implications

Our findings underscore the importance of treating "considerers" as a meaningful group. Failing to account for considerers risks obscuring meaningful variation among non-owners, misidentifying target audiences, and directing strategies toward the wrong people and/or levers of influence.

For example, messaging and services aimed at promoting legitimacy and reducing uncertainty may encourage considerers but backfire among existing owners, who may value risk-taking, novelty, and the notion of cryptocurrency as an oppositional tool. Conversely, messaging emphasizing exclusivity, sophistication, or financial autonomy may resonate with owners but discourage considerers, who may be more sensitive to subjective norms and negative control beliefs.

5.3. Limitations and Future Research

First, we rely on cross-sectional observational data, such that we cannot establish causality, the stability of considering, or how people might progress through adoption stages over time. Second, there may be additional heterogeneity among considerers. Third, our study is limited to US adults. Fourth, despite rigorous attention to survey design, our samples vary in self-reported ownership rates—Oaxaca–Blinder decompositions indicate these differences are partially attributable to sample differences in age.
